title: The Goodness of the Lord
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Goodness of The Lord __ Psalm 23 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: God as shepherd: OT imagery taken up by Christ in John 10
The Lord is my shepherd — I shall not want (v.1)
Green pastures, still waters, restoring the soul (vv.2-3)
The valley of the shadow of death: God's presence and protection (v.4)
The table prepared in the presence of enemies; the anointed head (v.5)
Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all my days (v.6)
summary: An exposition of Psalm 23, the beloved psalm of the divine shepherd. Connects the OT imagery of God as shepherd directly to John 10 where Jesus declares 'I am the Good Shepherd.' Traces the psalmist's experience of want-free provision, soul-restoring rest, guided righteous paths, fearless passage through the valley of death, overflowing abundance even among enemies, and the enduring confidence that divine goodness and mercy pursue the believer every day of life.
transcript:
This is the great imagery of God in the Old Testament — God as the shepherd. That imagery becomes important because Jesus takes it upon himself in the New Testament. Though Psalm 23 is not quoted anywhere in the New Testament, it is clearly behind several key references to God as the shepherd of his sheep, particularly in John 10, where Christ's ministry parallels the Lord's ministry in Psalm 23. In John 10:11, Jesus says, *"I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd gives his life for the sheep."* In Hebrews 13, Jesus is called the great shepherd of the sheep, pointing back to this Old Testament image. In 1 Peter 5, he is called the chief shepherd. All of this points to who God is in the Old Testament literature.

It is significant that the Messiah is often prophesied as a shepherd of the same kind as Yahweh — the same kind as the divine God — making him equal with God. In Ezekiel 34 — last week's homework; if you have not read it, this week is your chance — verse 31, God says to his people: *"You are my flock, the flock of my pasture; you are men, and I am your God."* This is in the context of the prophecy about the Messiah to come. And in Isaiah 40:11, which my wife Emily often sings to the children at night when they are afraid: *"He will feed his flock like a shepherd; he will gather the lambs with his arm and carry them in his bosom, and gently lead those who are with young."* All of these are references to the Messiah — to Jesus.

And yet the Bible also says that this is only true of Yahweh, the true God of the Bible. How can it be true of Jesus and also be exclusively true of the God of the Bible? There is only one answer: Jesus is that God we read about in the Old Testament. We must conclude that the Messiah who comes to be the shepherd of the sheep is not only from God but is God himself. The position that regards Jesus as something less than God is a very lazy position — because if you actually take the time to read the Bible in its fullness, it is very difficult to deny what the Bible teaches. You may disagree with the Bible, but it is clear what the Bible teaches.

David then tells us what God does as a shepherd. As a shepherd, God provides. As a shepherd, God guides. As a shepherd, God protects. That is what the shepherd does. That is what God does.

## The Lord Is My Shepherd: God Provides (vv.1-3)

It is important for us to understand rightly what it means when the Psalm opens: *"The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want."* What is it that God is providing here? Some have found in this verse a binding of God to give a Christian anything he wants. The Lord is my shepherd — I shall not want — therefore I want a new Mercedes, or a boat, or a plane, and God must give it to me. But the connotation of the word *want* here is much closer to the English word *need* or *necessity*. The Psalmist is rejoicing that Yahweh will not let him go without the necessities of life.

It is interesting that Bible translators have not dared to change such a well-known passage. It is clear that in any modern translation, this verse should probably read differently, but it is such a dear passage that translators conform to it. The same is true of John 3:16 — there are clearer ways to render it, but translators do not dare change the wording because it is so well-known.

The word *want* is further defined by the four actions of God in verses 2 and 3:

> *"He makes me to lie down in green pastures; he leads me beside the still waters. He restores my soul; he leads me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake."* (Psalm 23:2–3)

The first thing we see here is that God's provision is constant. In the ancient Near East, green pastures were seasonal. Shepherds had to keep moving the flock from pasture to pasture to find green grass. But here the shepherd *makes* the sheep to lie down. The great shepherd leads his sheep to where they do not have to keep moving to find satisfaction. He takes them to the place where satisfaction is always found — in his presence. His sheep can lie down knowing that his provision is not seasonal but constant.

Nowhere is that more clearly signified than in the weekly Lord's Day worship. Every Sunday the Lord meets with us. He feeds us through his Word. We pray together, read together, eat together, give together, and in that togetherness the shepherd is present with us and we can lie down in the green pastures of his presence.

God's provision is also thorough. Still waters refer to springs of fresh water or slow-moving streams. The idea is that a good shepherd knows where he is taking his flock. He is not going to lead the sheep to a place where the current could sweep them away. He takes them to a place where they can drink safely. We can be sure that what God provides will not harm us — it is for our good.

And God's provision involves the whole of life. The shepherd *restores* the soul. The word *soul* here refers to the whole person. The word *restore* carries the idea of refreshing or enjoyment — the same word is used in Proverbs 25:13: *"Like the cold of snow in time of harvest is a faithful messenger to those who send him, for he refreshes the soul of his masters."* That word *refreshes* translates the same Hebrew word as *restore* in Psalm 23. The Lord refreshes us. He provides continually for our souls. We might even say that God's provision renews us so that we feel that life in the presence of God is good and worth living. But we need to be in his presence to experience that — he gives enjoyment of life to those who trust in him.

## God Guides: The Paths of Righteousness (v.3)

David also says that God guides his people. He speaks of *the paths of righteousness*. These are not expressly paths that lead to righteousness — though eventually they will — but the right paths. The paths that are proper, good, the ones we should walk in. He leads us into what is right and good.

Throughout Scripture — and in particular in the book of Proverbs — two ways are set before us: the way of the Lord and the way of the world. The Lord will always lead us in the way that is right, his way. But we often choose not to follow him. Robert Frost has a poem about coming to a fork in the road, and choosing the road less traveled by. We often do that very thing — it is clear that God wants us to go one way, but in rebellion we choose otherwise. And then we wonder why things are falling apart. Brothers and sisters, when God clearly leads us in his Word that this is where you should go, we need to go that way.

## The Valley of the Shadow of Death (v.4)

Sometimes the right path that God leads us into will go through the valley of the shadow of death. I want to say something about this that might surprise you: this is not a song about dying. It is a song about living. The word *death* is actually not present in the original text the way we might expect. The phrase is better translated *the valley of deep darkness*. Dying is certainly one form of going through that valley of darkness — but there are many other times in our lives when we pass through it. It represents the most fearful darkness, the darkest experience. And that expression is used throughout Scripture in that broader sense.

God will at times take us through dark times — for our good, to purify us, to conform us to the image of his Son. It is striking that a song about the goodness of God tells us that God is leading us through the valley of the shadow of death, and that he is going to be with us there. God does not promise to eliminate darkness. He promises to walk with us in that darkness. As Isaiah 43 puts it — and I was discussing this over lunch just recently — God says: *When you pass through the waters, I will be with you; when you walk through the fire, you shall not be burned.* Not: I will spare you from the water. Not: I will remove you from the fire. He will be with you in it.

The rod and the staff will be with us — protection and guidance will be present with the people of God. Whether a path is hard or not does not determine whether it is the right path. The only question is: is this the path the Lord is leading me in? If it is, then it is the good path of the Lord, even if it is full of hardship.

## Provision, Guidance, and Protection: All from God (vv.4-5)

As we close our look at this portion of the Psalm, notice that according to David, all provision is God's provision — regardless of who brings it.

> *"Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil; for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies; you anoint my head with oil; my cup runs over."* (Psalm 23:4–5)

God provides. God leads us. The provision is from God. That is why we pray, *Give us this day our daily bread* — because God is providing for us today. We get anxious. Anxiety is usually made of two parts: we want to know tomorrow, and we want to control tomorrow. And yet God says, *give us this day our daily bread* — not tomorrow's bread, not next year's bread. Today. Focus on what God has you doing now. Tomorrow will take care of itself, because we have a shepherd who provides, who guides, and who protects his sheep as we walk even through the darkest valleys of our existence.
